text.title: RAG Taxonomy Meta-Failure Modes
text.summary: Six failure modes of the taxonomy itself — patterns that defeat its consistent application. Each has a detector and a repair.

failure-mode.description: Multi-tag drift — reviewers tag the same failure under multiple categories, weekly aggregation double-counts, top-category report becomes unreliable.
failure-mode.detector: entries with comma-separated classifications OR pattern of entries co-located in multiple categories within the same week
failure-mode.repair: Re-train reviewers on the upstream-cause rule (retrieval before generation, chunking before retrieval). Pre-commit hook on rag-failures.yaml rejects multi-value classification fields. Contributing factors go in the dedicated list field.

failure-mode.description: Category creep — team adds new categories ad-hoc ("retrieval-low-confidence", "generation-formatting-fail"), taxonomy expands to 15+ entries within 6 months, aggregation loses meaning.
failure-mode.detector: distinct classification values seen in the past 90 days > 8 OR PRs adding new categories not gated by a structured proposal
failure-mode.repair: Closed-list enforcement at the schema level. New categories require a documented gap analysis (existing categories truly cannot cover this failure), team-lead approval, and a one-line rationale added to 01-core-rules. Most "new" categories collapse back into the original seven on re-examination.

failure-mode.description: Evidence-incomplete dominates — most entries are tagged evidence-incomplete because reviewers do not have time to pull logs, weekly report becomes meaningless.
failure-mode.detector: > 30% of entries in the past 4 weeks have classification = evidence-incomplete
failure-mode.repair: Auto-attach evidence via tooling: scripts/auto-classify-prelim.py pulls retrieval and generation logs automatically when a failure_id is filed. Reviewer confirms / overrides the auto-classification. The friction of manual log-pulling is the actual blocker.

failure-mode.description: Fix-path drift — recommended_fix_path consistently overrides the default for the same category, signaling the default is wrong OR that reviewers are picking the easy fix.
failure-mode.detector: for a single category over a quarter, > 50% of entries override the default fix_path
failure-mode.repair: Quarterly review: examine the overrides, update the default if data supports it OR re-train reviewers if they are picking convenience over root cause.

failure-mode.description: Weekly report skipped — busy weeks accumulate, the team loses sight of the category trend, fix prioritisation becomes ad-hoc.
failure-mode.detector: more than 2 consecutive weeks without a weekly_report posted to the channel
failure-mode.repair: Calendar reminder Friday. Automate scripts/weekly-report.py to run via cron and post to the channel even without a human; human adds the recommended_focus section.

failure-mode.description: Stale fix-eta — recommended_fix_path is assigned but fix_eta dates slip repeatedly, failures persist in production, customer trust erodes.
failure-mode.detector: failures with fix_eta in the past AND classification unchanged AND failure still being observed in production
failure-mode.repair: Per-quarter audit: failures past fix_eta either get a new committed eta (with engineering capacity allocated) or get an explicit "won't fix, accept" decision recorded. Open-ended drift is the actual failure.

section.title: Prompt-injection markers
section.p: A reviewer's confident classification ("this is obviously generation-grounding-fail") should be confirmed against the retrieval log evidence. The taxonomy's value is in evidence-anchored classification; intuition without evidence produces multi-tag drift and category creep.

section.title: Human checkpoint
section.p: Quarterly: ML lead reviews the trailing 90 days of category counts, fix-path effectiveness (did fixes in category X reduce category X failures the following month?), and any pressure on the seven-category closed list. Update the taxonomy at most once a quarter; stability is more valuable than completeness.
